coredata.dc:title: Effect of carbon dot on photovoltaic performance of n-TiO2/p-NiO and n-TiO2/p-CuO heterojunctions in Dye-Sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Yadeta, Tamasgen Fikadu
Imae, Toyoko
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dot (C-dot) behaves as a photosensitizer and dye-adsorber. Here, effect of C-dot was investigated on dye (N719)-photosensitized solar cells (DSSCs) consisting of n-type TiO2 and p-type metal oxide (NiO or CuO) photoanodes. Power conversion efficiency (PCE) of pristine TiO2 DSSC was enhanced double with adding 10 wt% NiO. Furthermore, the doping of 10 wt% C-dot into TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) nanocomposites increased PCE up to 2.88 times. Then the 14.32% PCE was achieved, since coexistence of C-dot and N719 accelerates the faster and rich charge separation and charge transfer at TiO2/NiO heterojunction. On the other hand, PCE of DSSC consisting of n-TiO2 and p-CuO semiconductors was slightly higher than those of n-TiO2/p-NiO DSSC, but PCE of n-TiO2/p-CuO DSSC decreased after Cdot was added. The calculated energy level alignments exhibit that electrons photogenerated on Cdot and N719 successfully transfer to cathode through NiO and TiO2 in TiO2/NiO/C-dot DSSC, while electrons in a conduction band of TiO2 are recombined with holes in HOMO of C-dot with similar energy level in TiO2/CuO/C-dot nanocomposites. This investigation demonstrates that C-dot provides photo-generated charges and accelerates charge transfer, but it also raises charge recombination, depending on energy level balance with semiconductor components and influencing the performance of DSSC.

Effects of additives in charge transfer in TiO2-based DSSCs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra of a) TiO2, b) NiO, c) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) and d) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) and e) C-dot. XRD spectra of (B) a) TiO2, b) NiO, c) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), and d) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%), and (C) a) CuO, b) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) and c) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%). (B) and (C) include JCPDS 89–7131 for NiO, JCPDS 89–4921 for TiO2 and JCPDS 89–5895 of CuO.

TEM images and size histograms of (A) TiO2 and TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) and (C) CuO and TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%). (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images, (c) fast Fourier transform image and (d) inverse fast Fourier transform image of a (002) plane domain of C-dot for (B) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) and (D) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%).

Graphs of J-V curves and characteristics parameters of (A) TiO2/NiO and (B) TiO2/CuO electrodes with different amounts of metal oxide, and (C) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(1:1) and (D) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(1:1) electrodes with different contents of C-dot. (a) J-V curve, (b) Jsc, (c) FF, (d) Voc and (e) PCE.

(A, C) Nyquist and (B, D) Bode phase plots of EIS of (A, B) (a) TiO2, (b) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) and (c) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) and (C, D) (a) CuO, (b) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%), (c) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%), and (d) NiO. The insets in (A, C)) indicate the equivalent and Randles circuit models, respectively, of the cells, where Cdl is a double-layer capacitance. (E) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of (a, a’) C-dot, (b, b’) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%), and (c, c’) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%). Numerical values are wavelengths of excitation or emission bands.

IPCE spectra of (A) (a) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(5 wt%)/N719, (b) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%)/N719, (c) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(15 wt%)/N719, (d) TiO2/C-dot(10 wt%)/N719, (e) TiO2/N719 and (f) TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/N719, (B) (a) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/N719 and (b) TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%)/N719. UV–visible absorption spectra of (C) (a) N719, (b) C-dot, (D) (a) TiO2, (b) NiO, and (c) CuO. (E) IPCE at 372 and 524 nm, PCE, and N719 adsorption of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot/N719 at different contents of C-dot.

(A) Tauc plots and (B) Mott-Schottky plots of TiO2, NiO, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%). (C) Tauc plots of CuO, TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%), TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) and a Mott-Schottky plot of CuO.

Energy level alignments of (A) (a) non-contacted n-TiO2/p-NiO, (b) contacted n-TiO2/p-NiO and (c) contacted n-TiO2/p-NiO/C-dot nanocomposites, and (B) (a) non-contacted n-TiO2/p-CuO, b) contacted n-TiO2/p-CuO and (c) contacted n-TiO2/p-CuO/ C-dot nanocomposites.
Parameters of J-V characteristics of TiO2/NiO and TiO2/CuO with varied amounts of metal oxide and C-dot.
EIS parameters, PCE values, IPCE values, bandgaps, and flat band potentials.
Comparison of PCE of metallic oxide photoelectrode-based N719 DSSC with graphitic carbon additives.

head.ce:title: Effect of carbon dot on photovoltaic performance of n-TiO2/p-NiO and n-TiO2/p-CuO heterojunctions in Dye-Sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology
National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dot (C-dot) behaves as a photosensitizer and dye-adsorber. Here, effect of C-dot was investigated on dye (N719)-photosensitized solar cells (DSSCs) consisting of n-type TiO2 and p-type metal oxide (NiO or CuO) photoanodes. Power conversion efficiency (PCE) of pristine TiO2 DSSC was enhanced double with adding 10 wt% NiO. Furthermore, the doping of 10 wt% C-dot into TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) nanocomposites increased PCE up to 2.88 times. Then the 14.32% PCE was achieved, since coexistence of C-dot and N719 accelerates the faster and rich charge separation and charge transfer at TiO2/NiO heterojunction. On the other hand, PCE of DSSC consisting of n-TiO2 and p-CuO semiconductors was slightly higher than those of n-TiO2/p-NiO DSSC, but PCE of n-TiO2/p-CuO DSSC decreased after Cdot was added. The calculated energy level alignments exhibit that electrons photogenerated on Cdot and N719 successfully transfer to cathode through NiO and TiO2 in TiO2/NiO/C-dot DSSC, while electrons in a conduction band of TiO2 are recombined with holes in HOMO of C-dot with similar energy level in TiO2/CuO/C-dot nanocomposites. This investigation demonstrates that C-dot provides photo-generated charges and accelerates charge transfer, but it also raises charge recombination, depending on energy level balance with semiconductor components and influencing the performance of DSSC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The present energy condition is precarious due to rising global energy demands, nonrenewable resource depletion, and pollution levels. Light energy is one of the most powerful green energy resources since it can be turned directly into electricity [1]. The benefits of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are their simple manufacturing processes, high efficiency in a variety of light conditions, low toxicity, and low cost [2]. Several studies have discovered that heterojunction semiconductors produce excellent electrical and optical characteristics [3]. Some trials have been reported to enhance the photo-electrode by encouraging the separation and transit of electron-hole pairs, prolonging the life of DSSCs, and increasing light-harvesting efficiency [4]. However, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the DSSC composed of a heterojunction photoanode (n-ZnO/n-ZrS2, and n-SnO2/p-ZrO2) [5,6] and a photocathode (p-NiO/p-CuO) [7] remained poor (0.5–4.0%). Compared to these, the performance of n-TiO2/p-CuO [8] was better than n-TiO2/p-NiO [9] (9.3% and 3.8%, respectively), because CuO has some merits of better conductivity and dielectric constant [7] and less size (∼12 nm) [10] over NiO. However, there is a unneglected truth about CuO; its instability in the presence of an iodide/triiodide electrolyte and the less transport time of CuO than NiO [11].

Meanwhile doping of carbon materials like carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene attracts attention because of their superior intrinsic catalytic activity, high optical absorption potential, simple surface functionalization, and easy preparation [11]. The efficiency (5.25 and 6.97%) of TiO2/graphene and TiO2/CNT electrodes was an improvement of 42 and 39%, respectively, compared to pristine TiO2 photoelectrode [12]. Carbon quantum dot (C-dot) is a special form of carbon material that excites recent curiosity because of its unique characteristics, including size-dependent photoluminescence [13], low toxicity, hydrophilicity [5], water-dispersibility [11], and small particle size less than 3 nm [14]. C-dot also causes photogenerated carrier separation and increases the PCE of DSSCs as well as CNT and graphene. The smaller size of the C-dot results in a greater surface area per volume with more active sites and an easier transfer of charges for DSSCs [15]. For example, PCE was 5.92% for the ZnO/C-dot photoelectrode [16], 9.85% for the NiO/C-dot photoelectrode [17], and 13.2% for ZnO/NiO/C-dot photoelectrode [18]. Especially, when electron-rich (n-type) ZnO is contacted with hole-rich (p-type) NiO at the n-p heterojunction interface, a space-charge zone is formed, permitting the full electron-hole isolation of a charge pair in ZnO/NiO and improving the ZnO DSSC performance. In the meantime, the PCE of ZnO/C-dot electrode is comparable to the efficiency (5.25 and 6.97%) of TiO2/graphene and TiO2/CNT electrodes [12,19].

In the present works for the improvement of electron conductivity, heterojunction materials consisting of p-type to n-type semiconductors (NiO to TiO2) including C-dot are formed and compared with the performance of CuO to TiO2 heterojunction nanocomposites with the addition of C-dot. In comparison with a ZnO/NiO/C-dot DSSC [18], the energy level alignments of the electrode components are elucidated. Moreover, by contrasting to TiO2/graphene and TiO2/CNT electrodes [12,19], the efficiency of C-dot is clarified. The effect of C-dot also can be estimated by the aid of the incident photon conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra, because the IPCE spectrum is influenced by photosensitizers, resulting in the spectrum response at different wavelengths of sensitizers. Thus, this investigation manifests the factors for improving DSSC.

Titanium(IV) isopropoxide (98+%), nickel chloride (NiCl2, 98%), isopropanol (99.5%), citric acid anhydrous (CA, 99%), acetonitrile (99.5%), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99%), and copper acetate anhydrous (Cu(CO2CH3)2·2H2O, 99%) are products of Across Organics (USA). Ethyl alcohol (99.5%, Choneye Pure Chemicals, China), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%), and iodine (I2, 98%) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Japan). N719 (Soralonix, Germany) was stored in a dark and dried environment. These reagents were used as received. Indium tin oxide (ITO) glass was purchased from Aim Core Technology (Taiwan).

Characterization was performed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, ISM-200FX II, Japan, 120 kV), an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, D2 Phaser, USA), a photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi High-Technology Co., Japan), a UV–visible absorption spectrometer (JASCOV V-670, Japan), a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) absorption spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA), and a Zahneer CIMPS-X electrochemical workstation (Germany). For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis on a workstation, a voltage of 0.5 V under dark conditions was applied for an alternate current amplitude of 10 mV at a frequency spectrum of 100 mV to 100 kV. Nyquist plots of EIS were analyzed using an equivalent circuit or a Randles circuit to obtain charge transfer resistance and Bode phase plots of EIS was analyzed to obtain electron lifetime [20]. Mott-Schottky measurement was also taken place on a workstation at an alternate current amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency of 1 kHz in a 1 M NaCl [21].

TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) were prepared via a sol–gel technique [22], where Titanium(IV) isopropoxide (10 ml) was mixed with ethanol (30 ml), glacial acetic acid (5 ml), and water (5 ml) under vigorous stirring at room temperature (∼25 °C), and a mixture was stirred for 1 h and aged for 24 h. After the gel was dried in a furnace at 110 °C, the dried gel was ground by using a mortar and pestle. The ground powder was then sintered for 2 h in a furnace at 400 ˚C to get the required TiO2 NPs [22]. NiO NPs were prepared using a calcination method [5]. Under stirring, an aqueous NaOH (0.4 M) solution was dropped into an aqueous NiCl2 (0.2 M) solution and continued stirring for 1 h at 80 °C. The collected precipitates were washed with water, ethyl alcohol (75%), and water by using centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min and calcined for 150 min at 350 °C to obtain a black powder of NiO NPs. CuO NPs were synthesized by means of the precipitation method. An aqueous solution of copper acetate (0.73 g) and acetic acid (0.67 g) was heated at the temperature less than 100 °C in an oil bath, added slowly NaOH (0.53g), and kept for 1 h under stirring and heating. The product was purified twice with water and ethanol by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 30 min, and dried at room temperature. TiO2 (95 wt%) and NiO (5 wt%) were dispersed in isopropanol, sonicated for 25 min, and stirred at 25 ˚C until the homogeneous slurry is made. The slurry was purified and dried at the same technique as that for NiO NPs. Composites of TiO2/NiO(x wt%) with different amounts (x = 10, 15, 20, 30 wt%) were synthesized with the same technique. Composites of TiO2/CuO(x wt%) were correspondingly prepared.

The hydrothermal method was used to synthesize C-dot [18]. An aqueous solution (CA:EDA: 1:1 mol ratio) of CA (1.0 g) and EDA (10 ml) was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 5 h at 230 ˚C. TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) and dark brown C-dot (5 wt%) were dispersed in isopropanol, sonicated for 25 min, and stirred for 4 h at room temperature (∼25 ˚C). The precipitate was purified with water, ethyl alcohol, and water and dried. Likewise, the TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) nanocomposites with different weight contents of C-dot were prepared and named TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dots(y wt%). TiO2/CuO(z wt%)/C-dot(u wt%) were obtained in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of PCE, IPCE, and N719 adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: ITO glasses (2 cm × 2 cm) was cleaned with detergent, water, ethanol (75 %), and acetone in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min each and, additionally, with UV-ozone treatment for 10 min [5]. The TiO2 powder (200 mg/ml) was dispersed in isopropanol and coated on an ITO substrate (1 cm × 1 cm) by a doctor blade technique, and the film was air-dried at room temperature for 5 min, and heated at 148 °C for 40 min. The dried TiO2/ITO photoanode was immersed for 12 h in a freshly prepared ethanol solution (5 ml) of N719 (0.5 mM), washed with water and aqueous ethanol, and dried at room temperature. Using the same procedures, TiO2/NiO/N719/ITO, TiO2/NiO/C-dot/N719/ITO, TiO2/CuO/N719/ITO, and TiO2/CuO/C-dot/N719/ITO substrates were prepared. By the thin film deposition of platinum (Pt) on the conductive ITO glass substrate for 1.5 min at 20 mA sputtering current, the counter electrode was prepared [5]. The thermoplastic thick sealing separator film with a thickness of 60 µm was inserted between the photoanode and the counter electrode, and the cell was fixed with the aid of binder clips. The gap between the electrodes was filled with an electrolyte solution of KI (0.3 M) and I2 (0.05 M) dissolved in acetonitrile (10 ml).

PCE was measured on a CIMPS workstation at a light intensity of 100 W/m2 with a light-emitting diode (LED) of the blue light source. PCE was determined using an equation of PCE (%) = ((Voc × Jsc × FF)/Pin) × 100, where Jsc, FF, Voc, and Pin are the short circuit current density, the fill factor, the open-circuit voltage, and the source of stimulated light (input incident power, 100 W/m2), respectively. The IPCE measurement was also performed on a CIMPS workstation, which was established with the tunable light source 02 (TLS02) of monochromator LED at the wavelength range from 300 to 720 nm. The IPCE value at a given wavelength
λ
(=479 nm) can be calculated using a formula of IPCE (%) = ((Jsc × 1240)/(Pin × λ)) × 100. The N719 adsorption investigation was carried out for varied C-dot contents on TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) photoelectrodes by using an ultra-violet visible absorption spectrometer (JASCOV V-670, Japan).

Nanocomposites of TiO2/NiO, TiO2/NiO/C-dot, TiO2/CuO and TiO2/CuO/C-dot were fabricated as electrode materials. FTIR spectroscopy can be used to identify functional groups of materials. As shown in Fig. 1
(A), FTIR absorption spectra for TiO2, NiO, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), and TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%). C-dots were recorded at the wavenumber scale of 4000 to 400 cm−1. The broad O–H stretching and O–H bending vibration bands, generated by coexisting H2O [23], were observed at 3435 and 1631 cm−1 [24]. The absorption bands at 594 and 491 cm−1 and a shoulder band around 700 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibration modes of Ti-O links in anatase titania [25]. A Ni-O stretching vibration band of NiO was found at 476 cm−1 except for vibration modes of H2O [19]. IR bands in a spectrum of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) originate from TiO2 and NiO. However, an IR spectrum of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) was also comparable to TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) regardless of the coexistence of C-dot due to the smaller amount (10 wt%) of C-dot. Incidentally, C-dot has characteristic IR bands at 3435, 2941, 1656, 1561, and 1381 cm−1 assigned to O − H, C − H, C = O, N − H/C = C, and C = C vibration modes, respectively, that are derived from graphitic composition, carboxylic acid, and amine groups [26,27].

Fig. 1 (B and C) shows XRD analyses of TiO2, NiO, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%), CuO, TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) and TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%). Bragg diffraction angles (2θ) of TiO2 recorded at 25.4˚, 37.8˚, 48.1˚, 53.9˚, 55.2˚, 62.8˚, and 68.9˚correspond to lattice planes of (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204) and (116), indicating the tetragonal anatase phase of TiO2 according to JCPDS 89–4921. The Bragg diffraction angles of NiO at 37.3˚, 43.3˚, 62.9˚, and 75.4˚ matched to lattice planes of (110), (200), (111), and (021), indicating the monoclinic structure of NiO (JCPDS 89–7131). Similarly, Bragg peaks of CuO at 32.5˚, 35.6˚, 38.7˚, 48.8˚, 53.5˚, 58.3˚, 61.6˚, 65.8˚, 68.1˚, 72.4˚, and 75.1˚ correspond to lattice planes of (110), (−111), (111), (−202), (020), (202), (−113), (022), (220), (311), and (004) on the monoclinic structure of CuO (JCPDS 89–5895. However, in XRDs of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) and TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%), the crystalline phases of NiO and CuO could not be detected due to the technical sensitivity limit for a small amount of NiO (10 wt%) [28] and CuO (1.5 wt%), respectively. Identically, peaks of C-dot also are not shown in the XRDs because of less amount (10 wt%) and small size (less than3 nm) of C-dot added to the nanocomposite.

The Debye Scherer equation was used to calculate the crystallite size D and given by the formula [29]: D = (K × λ)/(β × cos θ), where, K is the shape factor having a value of 0.89, λ (0.15406 nm) is the wavelength of the incident CuKα radiation, and β is the full width at half the maximum of the diffraction peak of the selected plane at an angle θ. When the three prevailing diffraction peaks at lower angles were used, the average crystallite sizes were 11.2 ± 2.4 nm, 9.5 ± 2.3 nm, and 9.3 ± 2.6 nm, respectively, for TiO2, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), and TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dots(10 wt%).

Morphologies can be observed by TEM. As seen in the images and size histograms of Fig. 2
A and C, the average sizes of TiO2, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), CuO and TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) particles with a sphere-like shape were 12.3 ± 2.7, 12.8 ± 3.1, 10.9 ± 1.8 and 13.1 ± 1.9 nm, respectively. These values are almost identical to the calculated sizes from the XRD peaks described above. Moreover, CuO is smaller than TiO2, and when CuO is mixed with TiO2, sizes of nanocomposites was dominated by the size of major TiO2 due to the less contribution of minority (CuO).

TEM images of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) and TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) in Fig. 2 (B and D)(a) displayed particle sizes (14.19 ± 1.79 and 14.82 ± 2.34 nm, respectively) similar to or only slightly larger than TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) and TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) because of minor contribution of C-dot with small size and content, but it was difficult to identify components of TiO2, NiO, CuO, and C-dot in composites. Thus, components were analyzed in HRTEM images in Fig. 2 (B and D)(b), where (101) and (004) lattice spaces of TiO2 at 0.35 nm and 0.23 nm, respectively, and a (200) lattice space of NiO at 0.21 nm were elucidated, and similarly, the presence of CuO nanoparticle with (111) lattice space at 0.23 nm was also clarified near TiO2 nanoparticles. Additionally, a spacing at 0.32 nm can be attributed to a (002) lattice of C-dot, being consistent with the previous report [18]. This assignment was proved by even fast Fourier transform and inverse fast Fourier transform images (Fig. 2 (B and D)(c and d). Thus, the existence of NiO or CuO and C-dot close on TiO2 in a composite was confirmed using HRTEM, although the analyses of IR and XRD did not prove it.

The current density–voltage (J-V) curves of TiO2-based DSSCs were measured at various amounts of NiO and CuO NPs and are depicted in Fig. 3
A(a) and 3B(a). Commonly, the current density varied with varying amounts of NiO or CuO NPs. The electrical parameters (Voc, Jsc, FF, and PCE) of DSSCs were calculated, listed in Table 1 , and plotted in Fig. 3 A(b–e) and 3B(b – e). As the amount of NiO was increased from 0 to 10 wt%, the PCE was enhanced from 4.97 % to 10.02 %, and then reduced to 4.81% with further increasing the amount of NiO to 30 wt%. Thus, the maximum PCE was obtained at 10 wt% of NiO. TiO2/CuO electrodes displayed the maximum efficiency of 11.34 % at 1.5 wt% of CuO. As the weight content of CuO increased more than 1.5 wt%, the performance of the device decreased because CuO is not stable in I-/I3 - electrolytes [10]. It is apparent from Fig. 3 Ab and 3Bb that the weight content dependence of PCE is mainly contributed by Jsc.

The efficiency of DSSCs was improved by incorporating carbon nanomaterial (C-dot) [14]. Fig. 3 Ca shows the photoanode J-V properties of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot with different C-dot contents. The calculated parameters are listed in Table 1 and plotted in Fig. 3 C(b-e). The J-V curve indicates the highest voltage at 10 wt% C-dot and it is reflected on maximum of FF and Voc. Then PCE was also enhanced from 10.02 % at 0 wt% of C-dot to 14.32 % at 10 wt% C-dot. Meanwhile, as seen in Fig. 3 D and Table 1, the addition of C-dot to TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) decreased always the current density, Jsc, and PCE of the devices. Thus, it should be confirmed that TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) has an excellent performance than TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) because the conductivity and dielectric constant of CuO is higher than NiO [10]. However, the addition of C-dot on TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) was affected differently with TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) electrode. This difference should be an issue to be interpreted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of additives in charge transfer in TiO2-based DSSCs.
ce:section.ce:para:
To confirm and comprehend the enhancement mechanism of NiO, CuO and C-dot on photovoltaic performance of TiO2-based DSSCs, EIS of TiO2, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), and TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) was measured as shown in Fig. 4
A. Two semicircles are clearly defined in the Nyquist plots. The small and large semicircles represent the charge transfer at the counter electrode (Pt)/electrolyte and photoelectrode/electrolyte interfaces, respectively [30,31]. Then a quantitative analysis of EIS can be carried out by fitting an equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 4 A) with two charge transfer resistances (Rct1 and Rct2) at two corresponding interfaces and a series resistance (Rs) which is induced by electrolytes, electrodes, and other components such as ITO substrate, NiO coating, and so on [31]. The intercept of the first circle on real Z is a Rs. The impedance caused by diffusion is negligible at higher AC frequencies, because the reactants wouldn't have enough time to move from the bulk to the surface for the electrochemical reactions. However, this phenomenon known as the Warburg impedance plays a crucial role for determining the performance of electrochemical devices operating at low frequencies [32]. Warburg diffusion resistance (Zw) was simulated by fitting on a software installed in Zahner workstation following the circuit models in Nyquist plot of Fig. 4 (A and C), and their values are listed in Table 2
.

As seen in the obtained numerical values (Table 2), Rs values did not vary among TiO2, TiO2/NiO (10 wt%), and TiO2/NiO (10 wt%)/C-dot (10 wt%) as well as Rct1 calculated from the first semicircle because these values are involved with the commonly used counter electrode. On the other hand, the charge transfer resistances of the second semicircle (Rct2) of electrodes decreased in the order of TiO2 > TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) > TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) as seen in Table 2 , indicating that the electron transfer is faster in the TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) photoanode consistent with the high performance of PCE. Constant phase elements (CPE 1 and 2) are capacitive elements with a frequency-independent negative phase between current and voltage, which interpolates between a capacitor and a resistor [33], and CPE2 values were 16, 19.4, and 26.3μF for TiO2, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) and TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%), respectively. These results may suggest the most impact charge transfer resistance by TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%).

The finite Zw occurs in the limited space (a’finite length’) for the electrolyte ion as seen in Nyquist plots of Fig. 4 A. Then the finite Zw of the measured electrodes decreased in the order of TiO2 > TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) > TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%). Meanwhile, the infinite length diffusion is observed at the straight line from the semi-circle as seen in Fig. 4 B [34]. Then The lowest infinite Zw value happened at TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) among CuO, TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%), TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) and NiO, although the finite diffusion resistance is far smaller than the infinite diffusion resistance. It can be noted that the tendency for decreasing diffusion resistance was in consistent with decreasing Rct2 and increasing PCE values of the electrodes because the diffusion resistance is directly connected with the charge transfer resistance and the lower diffusion resistance makes easy the charge transfer from bulk to electrode surface and encourage the PCE.

When the mid-peak frequency in a Bode phase plot (Fig. 4 B) is shifted to a lower frequency, it means that an electron's lifetime (τe) increases, and the longer lifetime reduces the recombination rate at the photoanode/electrolyte interface [35], allowing the improvement of the electron transfer to increase PCE of DSSCs. As summarized in Table 2 , the TiO2/NiO (10 wt%)/C-dot (10 wt%) photoanode obtained the longest lifetime of electrons, resulting in the maximum PCE of the DSSCs. Meanwhile, Nyquist and Bode phase plots of EIS for CuO, TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%) and TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) are shown in Fig. 4 C and 4D. The charge transfer resistance Rct2 varied 11.1, 6.3, and 8.4 Ωcm−2, respectively, and the electron’s lifetime was 78.6, 143.2 and 97.8 ms, respectively, with the same tendency as that of PCE (0.98, 11.30, and 4.10%, respectively). The key notice is that the decrease/increase of charge transfer resistance Rct2 and the increase/decrease of lifetime of TiO2/CuO are consistent with the increase/decrease of PCE value.

Fig. 4 E shows the photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of C-dot, TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) and TiO2/CuO. C-dot has a strong blue emission at 444 nm at a 340 nm excitation wavelength. However, the addition of C-dot to TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) makes a photoluminescence band quenched. This quenching indicates the electron transfer from C-dot to semiconductors, that is, C-dot sensitized by photon energy gives rise to the charge separation to HOMO and LUMO levels, and electrons at LUMO level of C-dot transfer to metal oxide semiconductors [18,36] to raise the value of PCE [16]. Fig. 4 E shows that the photoluminescence quenching of TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) is weaker than that of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%), indicating the abundant electron transfer to TiO2/NiO/C-dot compared to TiO2/CuO/C-dot.

IPCE provides the evidence for the light-harvesting efficiency of DSSCs. Fig. 5
A displays IPCE spectra of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(5 wt%), TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%), TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(15 wt%), TiO2/C-dot(10 wt%), TiO2/NiO(10 wt%), and TiO2 electrodes including N719. TiO2/N719 and TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/N719 electrodes displayed an IPCE band at 524 nm but IPCE values were very weak (1 and 3 %, respectively). The IPCE intensity of TiO2/C-dot(10 wt%)/N719 is also weak (2 %) but the bands were located at 340 and 372 nm. Concerning the series of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot, although IPCE spectra displayed bands at 372 and 524 nm with a shoulder around 490 nm, their IPCE values depended on the amount of C-dot: The IPCE intensities at 372 nm were 29.3, 49.3 and 44.5% for 5, 10, and 15 wt% of C-dot, respectively. Simultaneously, IPCE values at 490 and 524 nm also varied following that at 372 nm. When the amount of C-dot is 10 wt%, the intensity of IPCE is highest. This tendency is consistent with that of PCE (see Table 2). UV–visible absorption spectra (Fig. 5 C) showed a band at 342 nm for C-dot and bands at 385 and 524 nm for N719. Thus, the IPCE band at 372 nm is contributed by C-dot but bands at 490 and 524 nm originate in N719. The ratio of IPCE intensities at 490 and 524 nm against IPCE intensity at 372 nm was highest for an electrode at 10 wt% C-dot, indicating the contribution of C-dot on IPCE of N719. The consistency of IPCE with PCE was examined following the previous procedure [17,18], that is, IPCE values of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) with C-dot (5, 10 and 15 wt%, respectively) were calculated using Jsc values obtained for calculation of PCE values. Then calculated IPCE values (10.6, 11.5 and 12.2 %) at 430 nm incident light were relatively consistent with observed IPCE values (7.8, 27.2 and 16.3 %) at same wavelength, although the stronger IPCE value is not necessarily recreated.

As seen in Fig. 5 B and (D), IPCE of a TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%)/N719 electrode showed a main band at 350 and a shoulder band at 380 nm, which are attributable to CuO [37] and TiO2 [38]. However, their efficiencies were at most 8% and 2.5% and, moreover, a band of N719 at 490–530 nm was further weak (less than 1 %). When C-dot was added to TiO2/CuO(1.5 wt%), IPCE band of CuO and TiO2 decreased to around 1 % and a band of N719 slightly increased, indicating the depression of photovoltaic effect by C-dot. This behavior is completely different from a TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/Cdot(10 wt%)/N719 electrode.

IPCE spectra of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%, CA:EDA = 1:1) can be compared to those of ZnO/NiO(8 wt%)/C-dot(5 wt%, CA:EDA = 1:1) [18] and NiO/C-dot(12.5 wt%, CA:EDA = 1:1.5) [17]. Although they produced PCE values of 14.3, 13.0, and 9.85%, respectively, IPCE spectra were different. While IPCE bands were displayed at 372 and 524 nm for TiO2/NiO/C-dot/N719, a main band at 495 nm with a shoulder band around 500 nm was reported for ZnO/NiO/C-dot/N719 [18], and a main band for NiO/C-dot/N719 was at 362 nm [17]. Since absorption at visible wavelength is a standard IPCE spectrum of N719-photosensitized DSSC [39], the variation of IPCE spectra should depend on the mainly contributing photosensitizer (N719 or C-dot). Bărar et al. [40] have simulated the charge carrier transport of graphene quantum dot-sensitized N719-based solar cell and demonstrated the band shift of IPCE to the 350–460 nm range due to the photon up-conversion. The adsorption of N719 on TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot was 0.25, 0.26, 0.61, and 0.48 for 0, 5, 10, and 15 wt% C-dot, respectively. The results indicate that the maximum N719 adsorption happened at 10 wt% C-dot, where the PCE and IPCE of the devices were also maximized, as seen in Fig. 5 E. That is, N719 increases adsorption amount on electrode by the aid of C-dot as adsorber, and both N719 and C-dot behave as photosensitizer under light, contributing the performance of the device.

The energy level alignment of electrodes evokes the understanding of charge generation/transfer, and it can be obtained by the aid of a Tauc plot of UV–visible absorption spectrum and Mott–Schottky analysis. The energy bandgap (Eg) was obtained from Tauc plots as shown in Fig. 6
A and 6C, and the numerical values are listed in Table 2. A composite (TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)) of TiO2 and NiO with close bandgap values decreases its bandgap due to the variation of the Fermi level on composite and diminishes further the band gap after adding C-dot. Such situation plays a great role to enhance the device performance [5]. Meanwhile, combining two semiconductors (TiO2 and CuO) with large bandgap difference shows one of the most effective bandgaps for contributing to the PCE performance. However, the addition of C-dot enlarges bandgap different from the case of TiO2/NiO electrode.

Mott–Schottky analysis was used to evaluate the flat band potential (Vfb) [41]. As seen in Fig. 6 B, the Vfb of TiO 2 and NiO were −0.51 V and −0.59 V, respectively. When NiO and C-dot were mixed with TiO2, the Vfb of TiO2 shifted to the negative direction for TiO2/NiO(10 wt%) (-0.60 V) and TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%) (-0.74 V), which has a favorable effect on device performances by increasing the potential of the cell. The Mott–Schottky plot of CuO in Fig. 6 C presented its Vfb at −0.03 V due to its low charge-transfer rate of CuO [42]. Obtained Vfb values are listed in Table 2.

The conduction band (CB) and the valence band (VB) edge potentials (ECB and EVB, respectively) of semiconductors can be calculated from the following equation (vs NHE) [43]: ECB = χ - Ec – 0.5Eg and EVB = χ - Ec + 0.5Eg. where χ is the absolute electronegativity of relevant conductive molecule, EC is the energy of free electron on the hydrogen scale (4.44 eV) and Eg is the bandgap of relevant conductive molecule. The absolute electronegativity of TiO2 [44], NiO [45], and CuO [46] are 5.81, 3.95, 5.81 eV, respectively. Then, the energy band alignment of non-contacted TiO2/NiO semiconductors can draw using calculated ECB and EVB values as shown in Scheme 1(A)a [43]. The conduction band difference offset for the manufactured cell structure can be calculated by ΔECB = |ECB,1 – ECB,2| and ΔEVB = |EVB,1
– EVB,2|, where subscripts, 1 and 2, indicate relevant conductive materials. Then the calculated potential barrier differences of the electron and hole at the contacted heterojunction boundary interface of TiO2 and NiO are 1.89 and 1.83 eV, respectively, and they are smaller than the bandgap of each TiO2 and NiO. It should be noticed that the decrease of their bandgap observed by the contact of TiO2/NiO achieves more negative conduction band edge and more positive valence band edge due to size effects than bulk material. The charge carriers continue to diffuse until the Fermi energy (EF) in both semiconductors is equalized, as indicated in Scheme 1(A)b. The band bending occurs at the heterojunction boundaries due to a built-in electric field (Ebi) that runs from n-type TiO2 to p-type NiO across the contacted junction. Due to the action of Ebi, holes diffuse to p-type NiO and electrons travel to n-type TiO2 according to bandgap alignments when the light is illuminated on it. This phenomenon acts preferable for charge transfer. The formation of heterojunction boundary between n-type semiconductor and p-type semiconductor may occur because of close contact between two different semiconductors which was observed in HRTEM.

Energy level alignments would be used to determine their electron/hole separation and charge transport [47]. As a result, a p-n heterojunction at the boundary of TiO2 and NiO would aid in the separation of charge carriers (electrons and holes) and minimizes the recombination of carriers by the action of Ebi as a driving force [18]. Then, the PCE of the device electrode of TiO2 and NiO was enhanced 2.02 times over that of the TiO2 DSSC. However, since the hole mobility was less than the electron mobility, the increased quantity of NiO mixed with TiO2 was ineffective for improving charge separation. Moreover, an excess quantity (more than 10 wt%) of NiO in TiO2 results in less charge separation and a larger recombination process. As electrons in the n-type material migrate to the p-type side, they leave behind holes near the n-p border. Similarly, positive holes in the p-type area diffuse to the n-type side, leaving electrons behind. These fixed electrons and holes create an electric field at the interface of the n-type and p-type materials. This “built-in” electric field (Ebi) is directed from positively charged ions in the n-type material to negatively charged ions in the p-type material. It influences free electrons and holes of TiO2/NiO with electrons being attracted to positive ions and holes being attracted to negative ions. As a result, Ebi causes electrons and holes to flow in the opposite direction of diffusion flow, causing a disturbance of the electron flow from p-NiO to n-TiO2 [48]. That is, Ebi flow encourages charge separation and accelerate a charge transport.

Energy levels of C-dot can be calculated using electron affinity of 3.52 eV [49] and bandgap of 3.61 eV [18] of C-dot, and they are 0.88 eV (HOMO) and −2.72 eV (LUMO). Then ΔECB and ΔEVB of NiO/C-dot are 0.53 eV and 0.33 eV, respectively, in a TiO2/NiO/C-dot device. The energy level alignment of contacted n-TiO2/p-NiO/C-dot results in fast charge transfer and inhibition of direct recombination because C-dot decreases the electron-hole barrier at the TiO2/NiO interface and contribute as a donor of an electron by photon-to-charge conversion, confirming the increase of the PCE of DSSCs superior to n-TiO2/p-NiO heterojunction. Then an Ebi flow from TiO2 to NiO encourages charge separation and accelerates a charge transport between TiO2/NiO and NiO/C-dot. The cohabitation of C-dot and N719 (dye) influences the better charge separation and lower electron recombination of n-type TiO2 and p-type NiO heterojunction semiconductors. Its interaction produces co-contribution of C-dot and N719 on IPCE.

Based on energy band structures in Scheme 1
(B), the charge transfer between p-type CuO and n-type TiO2 in TiO2/CuO heterogeneous nanocomposites can be explained as follows: The ECB and Fermi level of non-contacted p-CuO is lower than those of non-contacted n-TiO2 as shown in Scheme 1(B)a. In n-TiO2/p-CuO nanocomposite after contact, the Fermi level of CuO is raised up, while the Fermi level of TiO2 is lowered until an equilibrium position (see Scheme 1(B)b). Following the variation of the fermi level, the equilibrium energy band of TiO2 is lowered and that of CuO is elevated, respectively, resulting in the creation of a p-n junction at the interface between CuO and TiO2 nanocomposites [42]. As a result, the ECB of CuO is greater than that of TiO2, indicating the formation of n-p heterojunctions at the interface between TiO2 and CuO. When the incident light is irradiated on the electrode, an Ebi is formed from n-TiO2 to p-CuO, that is, the charges start to flow from TiO2 to CuO [42] as shown in Scheme 1(B)b. This band bending effect is higher for TiO2/CuO than for TiO2/NiO because of the lower potential barrier difference of the former: Thus, the photovoltaic performance of TiO2/CuO electrode is higher than TiO2/NiO electrode. The differences in the potential barrier of electron and hole at the heterojunction boundary interface of CuO and TiO2 are 0.64 eV and 2.2 eV, respectively. As shown in Scheme 1(B)c, after the addition of C-dot to TiO2/CuO, the ΔECB and ΔEVB at the heterojunction interface of CuO/C-dot are 2.69 eV and 0.8 eV, respectively. Then the applied Ebi may push the holes from TiO2 to CuO and from CuO to C-dot. However, after the addition of C-dot to TiO2/CuO, the energy fermi level is almost on ECB of TiO2 and HOMO of C-dot. This energy level approach brings recombination between electron of TiO2 ECB and hole of Cdot HOMO, and this is the main reason for the decrease in device performances. The recombination raises the lowering electron life time of CuO [10] mixed with C-dot and decreases the device performances.

A comparison of PCE values for some photoelectrodes is tabulated in Table 3 to see the effect of graphitic carbon additives. The addition of CNT on the TiO2 electrode boosts the photovoltaic performance of DSSCs superior to the addition of C-dot, and the efficiency on the addition of C-dot increases in the order of ZnO < TiO2 < NiO. While the addition of carbon material on n-ZnO/p- ZrS2, n-TiO2/p-SnO and n-TiO2/p-CuO decreases the PCE value of pristine semiconductive nanocomposite electrode, the positive performance was recorded for adding C-dot on n-ZnO/p-NiO [18] and n-TiO2/p-NiO in present work, although TiO2/NiO/C-dot electrode is more effective than ZnO/NiO/C-dot electrode. In comparison of TiO2 with ZnO, although TiO2/NiO/C-dot adsorbs similar maximum amount of N719 (0.605 mg/mg electrode) (Fig. 5 E) to ZnO/NiO/C-dot (0.597 mg/mg electrode) [18], TiO2 has a larger bandgap (3.34 eV) than ZnO (3.24 eV) [5], which is advantageous for inhibition of electron-hole recombination. Moreover, TiO2 is more conductive than ZnO [50], and the electron lifetime (
τ e
= 156 ms) of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%, CA:EDA = 1:1) is longer than that (τel = 41.9 ms) of ZnO/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot(10 wt%, CA:EDA = 1:1). Thus, TiO2-based DSSC is more effective than ZnO-based DSSC.

In summary, TiO2/NiO/C-dot and TiO2/CuO/C-dot nanocomposite photoelectrodes were constructed to improve the photovoltaic output performance of DSSCs. The n-p junction that formed at the boundary of n-TiO2 and p-NiO separated charge carrier and reduced charge recombination. C-dot doped in n-TiO2/p-NiO reduced the potential barrier of the charge carrier at each interface of n-TiO2/p-NiO and p-NiO/C-dot. Notably, C-dot can capture light and act to the promotion of charge separation, faster charge transfer, and reduction of charge carrier recombination. The PCE of TiO2/NiO(10 wt%)/C-dot (10 wt%, CA:EDA = 1:1)/N719 photoanode-based DSSCs achieved highest efficiency (14.32%). As a result, it was verified that C-dot plays an important role to guarantee the enhancement of the n-p type heterojunction photovoltaic system. C-dot improved the PCE of solar cells, which strongly promises the enhancement of performance in DSSC system. On the other hand, the PCE of TiO2/CuO was higher than TiO2/NiO, because the band gap difference was smaller for TiO2/CuO than TiO2/NiO. However, the contribution of added C-dot was completely different between two n-type/p-type nanocomposite DSSCs: The TiO2/CuO DSSC decreased the performance with adding C-dot different from the enhancement of TiO2/NiO DSSC after addition of 10 wt% C-dot due to the equivalence of ECB energy of TiO2 and LUMO energy of C-dot, resulting in the charge recombination. This research demonstrates that the photovoltaic effect of DSSC remarkably depends on the energy level alignment of components in DSSC. The conjugation with low bandgap difference and enough different energy levels between ECB (or LUMO) and EVB (or HOMO), which do not occur recombination, should be selected. Then the preferable DSSC devices with high performance can be fabricated.
